chunks.text: Akash Tomy is a backend-focused full stack developer from Alappuzha, Kerala. He builds reliable systems with a focus on concerns like race conditions, cache invalidation, transactional integrity, query optimization, and async workers. He is actively looking for full-time backend, full-stack, or AI infrastructure roles.

aliases: what roles is he looking for
chunks.text: Akash Tomy is based in Alappuzha, Kerala, India. He is actively looking for full-time roles (remote or on-site) in backend engineering, Django development, full-stack product engineering, or AI infrastructure.

Akash Tomy completed his Bachelor of Computer Application (BCA) from Christ College Pulliyanmala, Kattapana, Idukki, Kerala from 2022 to 2025. His CGPA is 7.48. Relevant coursework includes Data Structures, DBMS, Cloud Computing Basics, and Design and Analysis of Algorithms.

Akash Tomy worked as a Python Full Stack Developer Intern at SMEC Technologies from July 2024 to present. He reduced page-load database queries on high-traffic catalog and auth pages by adding Redis caching, developed reusable React components, designed normalized MySQL schemas, deployed a Django application to AWS EC2, and participated in daily code reviews.

Akash's highlighted projects are EasyBuy (a production e-commerce site), AI Project Judge (a planned repository evaluation system), and AI Akash (this portfolio chatbot). These projects demonstrate his skills in backend development, system design, and AI integration.

AI Project Judge is Akash's upcoming flagship engineering project. It is an AI-assisted repository evaluation system for hackathons, portfolios, and hiring screens. It aims to produce grounded, auditable evaluations.

The goal of AI Project Judge is to move beyond naive LLM-based code scoring. Instead of just asking a model to guess a score, the system combines deterministic static analysis with LLM interpretation to provide a more reliable and fact-based evaluation.

AI Project Judge is planned with a FastAPI backend, Celery workers for evaluation tasks, Redis as a broker, PostgreSQL for storage, and a React frontend. It will use Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time updates.

The AI Akash portfolio chatbot is a RAG system with a FastAPI backend. It uses Gemini for embeddings and generation, ChromaDB for vector storage, and a hybrid retrieval strategy (vector + keyword) to find the most relevant information to answer questions.

EasyBuy is a full-stack e-commerce platform with customer, seller, and admin roles. It is deployed on AWS and serves as a practical demonstration of Akash's backend skills in a production-like environment.

Akash built EasyBuy to gain hands-on experience with the real-world challenges of backend development that go beyond simple CRUD apps. The project was a vehicle for learning about payment integration, concurrency control, performance optimization with caching, and cloud deployment.

EasyBuy is built with a Django backend, React frontend, MySQL on AWS RDS, Redis for caching and message brokering, Celery for async tasks, Nginx as a reverse proxy, and Gunicorn as the application server. Payments are handled by Razorpay.

Measured backend improvements on EasyBuy include reducing database queries on a key page from 36 to 21 (a 42% reduction) using ORM optimization and keeping order confirmation response times under 300ms by offloading notification jobs to Celery workers.

A critical challenge in e-commerce is preventing overselling. In EasyBuy, this was solved by using a database-level row lock (`select_for_update`) within an atomic transaction. This ensures that checking and updating stock is an indivisible operation, guaranteeing inventory integrity under concurrent load.
Akash's primary programming language is Python for backend development. He also uses modern JavaScript (ES6+) for frontend work with React.

To handle race conditions in concurrent environments, Akash uses database-level locking mechanisms like `select_for_update` and wraps critical operations in atomic transactions. This is crucial for features like inventory management to prevent overselling.
Akash Tomy speaks English, Malayalam, Hindi, and Tamil.

Akash's main strength is his focus on systems thinking beyond just the happy path. He pays attention to production concerns like concurrency, performance, deployment, and cost. He's not just trying to make features work; he's trying to make them work reliably and efficiently.

A key learning experience for Akash was debugging e-commerce race conditions. It taught him that some problems only appear under concurrent load and led to a deeper understanding of database transactions and locking. It highlighted the importance of designing for concurrency from the start.

Akash uses Django for its 'batteries-included' philosophy, which is excellent for building complex applications like e-commerce platforms quickly and securely. The built-in ORM, admin, and authentication provide a robust foundation, letting him focus on business logic.

Akash uses FastAPI for services that require high performance and asynchronous I/O, like the AI Project Judge. Its modern features, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and Pydantic data validation make it ideal for building robust, high-throughput APIs.

Akash uses Celery to handle asynchronous tasks because it's crucial for good web performance. For an action like placing an order, the user should get an immediate response. Slow side-effects like sending emails can be offloaded to a Celery worker, keeping the web server responsive.

Akash uses Redis for two main purposes: as a fast in-memory cache to reduce database load for frequently read data, and as a message broker for Celery. Its speed and versatility make it a standard tool for improving the performance and scalability of web applications.

For AI Project Judge, PostgreSQL was chosen over MySQL because of its superior support for complex queries and data types, particularly its robust JSONB capabilities. This is ideal for storing the structured, semi-structured results from AI evaluations and static analysis.

For EasyBuy, MySQL was chosen because it's a reliable, widely-used relational database that is well-supported by Django and AWS RDS. For standard e-commerce transactional data (users, products, orders), its performance and simplicity are an excellent fit.

The portfolio chatbot uses Google's Gemini models for embeddings and generation. Gemini offers a strong combination of performance, a generous free tier for a portfolio-scale project, and a simple, well-documented API, making it a practical choice.

ChromaDB was chosen as the vector store for this RAG system because it's open-source, lightweight, and can be run in-process or as a standalone server. For a small to medium-sized knowledge base, it's very easy to set up and provides all the necessary functionality for embedding storage and retrieval.

A common but tricky bug I've debugged is a CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) error. The frontend on `akashtomy.com` was blocked from calling the backend API. The fix was in the FastAPI backend, ensuring the `CORSMiddleware` was configured to explicitly allow the frontend's origin. It's a reminder that browser security rules are strict and require correct server-side configuration.

I once faced a 502 Bad Gateway error after a deployment. After checking Nginx logs, I found it couldn't connect to the Gunicorn socket. The issue was that the Gunicorn service failed to start because of a syntax error in a settings file. This taught me to always check the application service status (`systemctl status gunicorn`) first before debugging the web server.

Debugging Razorpay webhooks was challenging. Orders were not being marked as paid because the HMAC signature verification was failing. The root cause was subtle: the raw request body was being parsed by middleware before my verification function could access it, altering the content used for the signature. The fix was to access the raw, unparsed request body directly to compute a matching signature.

In a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, builds were slow because the dependency cache wasn't working. I found the cache key was too specific, including commit hashes, causing a cache miss on every run. I changed the key to be based only on the `requirements.txt` lockfile. Now, the cache is only invalidated when dependencies actually change, speeding up deployments significantly.
Akash's email is akashtomy174@gmail.com.
